title: Network Address Translation (NAT)
description: Translates private IP addresses to public IPs — extends IPv4 address space and provides implicit firewall.
fullExplanation: NAT allows multiple devices with private IP addresses to share a single public IP address. The NAT router maintains a translation table mapping (private IP, private port) → (public IP, public port). When a packet leaves, NAT rewrites the source IP/port. When a reply arrives, NAT rewrites the destination IP/port back to the private address. PAT (Port Address Translation), also called NAPT or 'NAT overload', is the most common form — it uses port numbers to distinguish connections from different private hosts.

types.description: One-to-one mapping: one private IP always maps to one public IP. Used for servers.
Pool of public IPs assigned dynamically to private hosts. Less common.
Many private IPs share one public IP using port numbers. Most common (home routers).
Translates IPv6 addresses to IPv4 — enables IPv6-only hosts to reach IPv4 internet.

keyFacts: PAT allows ~65,535 simultaneous connections per public IP (one per port)
NAT breaks end-to-end connectivity — inbound connections require port forwarding
NAT is NOT a security feature — it's a side effect that some treat as one
NAT traversal: STUN/TURN/ICE protocols help P2P apps (VoIP, WebRTC) punch through NAT
Double NAT: two NAT layers (ISP + home router) — causes issues with gaming, VPN
IPv6 eliminates need for NAT — every device gets a public IP
NAT64 + DNS64: enables IPv6-only networks to reach IPv4 servers
